title: Consumers Expect Higher Household Income Growth and Report Slight Improvement in Credit Availability

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York released its September 2014 Survey of Consumer Expectations showing median household income growth expectations rose to 2.9% from 2.5% in August, a new 16-month high. Consumer inflation expectations remained stable at 3.0% at both the one-year and three-year horizons. Credit availability perceptions improved slightly relative to one year ago, though year-ahead credit expectations were unchanged.
key_points: Median household income growth expectations increased to 2.9% in September 2014, up from 2.5% in August—a new 16-month high
Median inflation expectations at both one-year and three-year horizons remained stable at 3.0% in September 2014
Median home price change expectations held at 3.5%, the lowest level since the data series began in June 2013
Mean perceived probability of job loss in the next 12 months increased to 16.6%, returning to levels observed in the first half of 2014
Median household spending expectations declined to 4.4%, reaching the lowest level since the data series began in June 2013
Perceptions of current credit availability relative to one year ago improved slightly, though expectations about year-ahead credit availability remained unchanged
why_it_matters: Consumer expectation surveys provide forward-looking sentiment indicators that can inform macroeconomic projections. Rising income expectations and improving credit perceptions suggest modestly improving household financial conditions, though the data is historical and from 2014—limiting its relevance to current market conditions.
macro_context: The SCE data captures household-level expectations for inflation, income, spending, and credit access. Income growth expectations at a 16-month high suggest improving consumer sentiment, while stable inflation expectations at 3.0% indicate persistent but unchanged inflation concerns. The slight improvement in credit availability perceptions contrasts with unchanged year-ahead credit expectations, suggesting households see recent easing but remain uncertain about future access. However, this data is from September 2014 and does not reflect current market conditions.
important_numbers: 16.6% mean perceived probability of job loss in next 12 months

issues_found: Source published October 14, 2014—over 11 years before TODAY (2026-04-10), with no clear archival value for current market structure or macro analysis
Consumer expectations survey data from September 2014 is not a 'historical policy decision' or 'key research finding' that warrants archiving given its age
Source has no direct relevance to current market conditions—the record itself acknowledges 'limiting its relevance to current market conditions'
Source_type in metadata is 'research_paper' but source content is a press release; however, this is a lower-tier issue
TARGET classification in source metadata is 'market structure' which appears incorrect—this is consumer sentiment survey data, more appropriate for macro catalysts

human_review.notes: The record is factually accurate and properly sourced, but the source is severely stale (11+ years old) with no clear archival value. Per CORE RULE 7 exception, age alone doesn't disqualify records with 'clear archival value' (historical policy decisions, key research findings), but consumer sentiment survey data from September 2014 does not meet that threshold. The record itself acknowledges the data 'does not reflect current market conditions.' This is not a borderline case requiring human judgment.

notes: Historical consumer sentiment survey from September 2014 released by NY Fed. This is a data release of the Survey of Consumer Expectations (SCE), not a market structure document. The TARGET tag of 'market structure' appears incorrect—this should be classified as macro catalysts data. Source is over 11 years old (2014 vs 2026-04-10 TODAY) and has no direct applicability to current market structure or macro catalysts analysis. Kept brief due to age and low utility.

reasoning: Severely outdated data from 2014—over 11 years old relative to TODAY
Survey data of limited direct relevance to current market structure or macro catalysts
Incorrect TARGET classification in source metadata
No direct market impact language in source; historical consumer sentiment data only
